fa: Involves persistent, excessive worry about everyday events that is difficult to control
ds: Formally named in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 3 in 100 adults in a given year
her: Moderate genetic component, alongside environmental factors
dl: Persistent worry can interfere with concentration, sleep and decision-making

fa: Characterized by persistent low mood and loss of interest in activities, one of the most common mental health conditions worldwide
ds: Described in various forms since antiquity; modern criteria set in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 5 in 100 adults globally each year
her: Moderate to substantial genetic component
dl: Can significantly reduce energy, motivation and ability to function at work or home

fa: A neurodevelopmental condition involving patterns of inattention, hyperactivity or impulsivity
ds: First clinically described by physician George Still in 1902
pr: Affects roughly 5-7 in 100 children, with many cases persisting into adulthood
her: One of the most heritable psychiatric conditions known
dl: Can affect focus, organization and impulse control at school, work and in relationships

fa: Involves intrusive, repetitive thoughts paired with compulsive behaviors performed to reduce the anxiety those thoughts cause
ds: Described in psychiatric literature since the 17th century; named OCD in the 20th century
pr: Affects roughly 1-2 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Compulsions can consume significant time each day and disrupt daily routines

fa: Marked by episodes of full mania alongside episodes of depression
ds: Concept traces to Emil Kraepelin's 'manic-depressive insanity' classification, 1899
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Strong genetic component, one of the most heritable mood disorders
dl: Manic episodes can disrupt work, relationships and judgment; depressive episodes can be incapacitating

fa: Involves episodes of hypomania, a milder form of elevated mood, alongside depressive episodes
ds: Formally distinguished from Bipolar I in the DSM-IV, 1994
pr: Affects roughly 0.5-1 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Strong genetic component
dl: Hypomanic episodes are less disruptive than full mania, but depressive episodes can still significantly affect daily life

fa: Involves intense fear of being judged or embarrassed in social situations
ds: Formally added to the DSM-III as 'social phobia', 1980
pr: Affects roughly 7 in 100 adults in a given year
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Can lead to avoidance of school, work meetings or social gatherings

fa: Characterized by recurring, unexpected panic attacks involving sudden intense fear and physical symptoms like a racing heart
ds: Formally distinguished from generalized anxiety in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 2-3 in 100 adults over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Unpredictable panic attacks can lead to avoidance of everyday places or situations

fa: Involves fear of situations where escape might be difficult, often leading a person to avoid certain public places
ds: Term coined by psychiatrist Carl Westphal in 1871
pr: Affects roughly 1-2 in 100 adults over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component, often overlapping with panic disorder
dl: Can severely limit travel and independence in more severe cases

fa: Can develop after experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event, involving intrusive memories and heightened alertness
ds: Formally added to the DSM-III in 1980, following observation of combat trauma in earlier wars
pr: Affects roughly 6-7 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component influencing vulnerability after trauma exposure
dl: Intrusive memories and heightened alertness can disrupt sleep, relationships and work

fa: Involves a chronic, lower-grade depressed mood lasting two years or more
ds: Named 'dysthymia' in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 1.5-2 in 100 adults
her: Moderate genetic component, overlapping with major depression
dl: Chronic low mood can subtly erode motivation and enjoyment over years

fa: A pattern of depressive episodes that follow a seasonal cycle, most often during darker winter months
ds: First formally described by psychiatrist Norman Rosenthal in 1984
pr: Affects roughly 1-5 in 100 people depending on latitude
her: Some genetic component related to light sensitivity and mood regulation
dl: Can reduce energy and motivation specifically during darker months

fa: Involves persistent, distressing preoccupation with perceived flaws in physical appearance that others often don't notice
ds: First described by Italian physician Enrico Morselli in 1891
pr: Affects roughly 2 in 100 people
her: Moderate genetic component, overlapping with OCD
dl: Preoccupation with appearance can consume hours daily and limit social situations

fa: Involves persistent difficulty discarding possessions, regardless of their actual value
ds: Recognized as a distinct diagnosis only in the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Affects roughly 2-6 in 100 people
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Cluttered living spaces can create safety hazards and strain relationships

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of spiders
ds: Described informally for centuries; formalized under specific phobia criteria in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Among the most common specific phobias, affecting a notable share of the population
her: Some evolutionary and genetic component proposed by researchers
dl: Can cause avoidance of gardens, basements or outdoor activities

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of heights
ds: Term coined by Italian physician Andrea Verga in 1887
pr: Affects roughly 2-5 in 100 people to a clinically significant degree
her: Some genetic component alongside learned experience
dl: Can limit activities like climbing stairs, flying or visiting tall buildings

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of enclosed or confined spaces
ds: Term coined in the late 19th century from Latin roots meaning 'to shut' and fear
pr: Affects roughly 2-4 in 100 people
her: Some genetic component alongside learned experience
dl: Can make elevators, MRI scans or crowded spaces distressing to endure

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of flying
ds: Formalized as a specific phobia category in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 10 people to some clinically relevant degree
her: Some genetic component, often linked to other anxiety conditions
dl: Can prevent air travel entirely or require significant coping strategies

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of water
ds: Formalized as a specific phobia category in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects a notable minority of people to a clinically significant degree
her: Some genetic component alongside learned experience
dl: Can prevent swimming, boating or beach activities

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of snakes
ds: Described informally for centuries; formalized under specific phobia criteria in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Among the most common animal-related phobias
her: Some evolutionary and genetic component proposed by researchers
dl: Can cause avoidance of hiking, gardens or rural areas

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of clowns
ds: Term popularized in the late 20th century, though not an official DSM diagnostic label
pr: Prevalence not precisely established, but commonly reported informally
her: Not clearly hereditary; often linked to childhood experiences or media
dl: Can cause distress at circuses, parties or media featuring clowns

fa: An aversion to clusters of small holes or bumps, though its formal classification as a phobia is still debated by researchers
ds: Term first proposed informally online in 2005
pr: Prevalence not precisely established; formal clinical recognition is still debated
her: Unclear; researchers debate whether it is evolutionary, learned, or not a true phobia
dl: Can cause discomfort when viewing common objects like sponges or honeycomb

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of public speaking
ds: Term derived from Greek roots, gaining wider use in the 20th century
pr: Frequently cited as one of the most common fears reported in public surveys
her: Not strongly hereditary; largely linked to social anxiety and experience
dl: Can affect school presentations, job interviews and career advancement

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of darkness
ds: Term derived from Greek roots for 'night'; especially common in childhood
pr: Very common in young children, decreasing significantly with age
her: Not strongly hereditary; largely developmental and experience-based
dl: Can disrupt bedtime routines and sleep, especially in children

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of vomiting
ds: Formalized as a specific phobia category in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 2 in 100 people to a clinically significant degree
her: Some genetic component alongside learned experience
dl: Can lead to restrictive eating or avoidance of illness-related situations

fa: A specific phobia involving intense fear of thunder and lightning
ds: Term derived from Greek roots for 'lightning'
pr: Common in childhood, less frequently persisting into a clinically significant adult phobia
her: Not strongly hereditary; largely developmental
dl: Can cause distress during storms and disrupt sleep or daily activities

fa: An eating disorder characterized by restriction of food intake and an intense fear of weight gain
ds: First clinically described by physician William Gull in 1873
pr: Affects roughly 0.5-1 in 100 people, predominantly diagnosed in women though it affects all genders
her: Substantial genetic component alongside social and psychological factors
dl: Can cause serious nutritional deficiencies and preoccupy daily thinking around food

fa: Involves cycles of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as excessive exercise or purging
ds: First formally described by psychiatrist Gerald Russell in 1979
pr: Affects roughly 1-2 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Substantial genetic component alongside social and psychological factors
dl: Binge-purge cycles can dominate daily routines and take a physical toll over time

fa: Involves recurrent episodes of eating unusually large amounts of food with a sense of loss of control
ds: First described by researcher Albert Stunkard in 1959; formally recognized as a diagnosis in the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Affects roughly 2-3 in 100 people, the most common eating disorder overall
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Recurrent binge episodes can affect physical health and daily eating routines

fa: Involves limiting food intake or variety in a way that causes nutritional or health problems
ds: Formally recognized as a distinct diagnosis in the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Prevalence not precisely established; more commonly diagnosed in children
her: Some genetic component, often overlapping with sensory sensitivities
dl: Can limit nutritional variety enough to affect growth or daily energy levels

fa: An eating disorder involving persistent eating of non-nutritive substances, with several recognized sub-types described below
ds: Named from the Latin word for magpie, a bird thought to eat indiscriminately; described in medical texts for centuries
pr: More common in children, pregnant women and certain nutritional deficiencies
her: Not strongly hereditary; often linked to nutritional or developmental factors
dl: Can pose physical health risks depending on the substance consumed regularly

fa: A common form of pica involving compulsive eating of ice, sometimes linked to iron deficiency
ds: Documented in medical literature since the 19th century
pr: One of the most commonly reported forms of pica
her: Not hereditary; frequently linked to iron deficiency
dl: Usually low physical impact, though frequent ice chewing can affect dental health

fa: A form of pica involving eating of soil or clay, documented across many cultures throughout history
ds: Documented across many cultures throughout recorded history
pr: More common in certain regions and during pregnancy in some populations
her: Not hereditary; often culturally or nutritionally influenced
dl: Can carry risk of parasitic infection or toxin exposure depending on the soil consumed

fa: A form of pica involving eating of hair, which in rare cases can lead to a hairball-like mass in the digestive tract
ds: Documented in medical case reports since the 19th century
pr: Relatively rare even among pica cases
her: Not hereditary; often associated with hair-pulling behaviors
dl: In persistent cases can lead to a hairball-like mass requiring medical attention

fa: A form of pica involving compulsive eating of starch, such as raw cornstarch or flour
ds: Documented in medical literature, particularly in relation to pregnancy
pr: Reported more frequently during pregnancy than in the general population
her: Not hereditary; often linked to pregnancy or nutritional factors
dl: Excessive intake can affect nutrition and blood sugar regulation

fa: A common, generally harmless behavior involving eating of one's own nasal mucus, which some studies have speculated may have a minor immune benefit
ds: Informally documented; not a distinct clinical diagnosis in most manuals
pr: Widely reported as a common habit, especially in childhood
her: Not hereditary; behavioral in nature
dl: Generally minimal impact on daily life or health

fa: Involves persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity to sleep
ds: Sleep difficulties documented throughout history; formalized as a distinct disorder in modern sleep medicine in the 20th century
pr: Affects roughly 10 in 100 adults chronically, with many more experiencing it occasionally
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Can impair concentration, mood and daytime functioning

fa: A neurodevelopmental condition involving differences in social communication and patterns of behavior or interests
ds: First described by psychiatrist Leo Kanner in 1943, with related work by Hans Asperger in 1944
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 100 children, per recent global estimates
her: Substantial genetic component, among the most heritable neurodevelopmental conditions
dl: Impact varies widely, from needing significant daily support to living fully independently

fa: A neurological condition involving repeated involuntary movements or vocalizations known as tics
ds: First described by neurologist Georges Gilles de la Tourette in 1885
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 100 school-age children, often improving by adulthood
her: Substantial genetic component
dl: Tics can range from barely noticeable to significantly disruptive in social or work settings

fa: An anxiety-related condition in which a person consistently cannot speak in specific social situations despite speaking normally elsewhere
ds: First described by physician Adolf Kussmaul in 1877 as 'aphasia voluntaria'
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 140 young children
her: Some genetic component, often linked to anxiety disorders
dl: Can significantly affect participation in school and social settings

fa: Involves emotional or behavioral symptoms that develop in response to a specific stressful life event
ds: Formally introduced as a diagnostic category in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: One of the most commonly diagnosed conditions in outpatient mental health settings
her: Not strongly hereditary; primarily triggered by external stressors
dl: Symptoms are usually temporary but can disrupt daily functioning during the adjustment period

fa: A severe form of premenstrual syndrome involving significant mood symptoms in the days before menstruation
ds: Added as a formal diagnosis in the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Affects roughly 3-8 in 100 menstruating individuals
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Can significantly affect mood and functioning in the days before menstruation each cycle

fa: A depressive episode occurring after childbirth, distinct from brief 'baby blues'
ds: Described informally for centuries; formally studied in modern psychiatry from the 1960s onward
pr: Affects roughly 10-15 in 100 new parents
her: Moderate genetic component alongside hormonal and psychosocial factors
dl: Can affect bonding with a newborn and daily caregiving capacity

fa: Involves excessive fear or anxiety about being separated from attachment figures
ds: Formally recognized in the DSM-III, 1980, initially as a childhood-specific diagnosis
pr: Affects roughly 4 in 100 children
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Can interfere with school attendance and sleeping away from attachment figures

fa: Involves persistent worry about having or developing a serious illness despite minimal or no actual symptoms
ds: Introduced in the DSM-5, 2013, replacing most cases previously called hypochondriasis
pr: Affects roughly 1-5 in 100 people
her: Moderate genetic component, overlapping with other anxiety conditions
dl: Frequent health worries can lead to repeated medical visits and daily preoccupation with symptoms

fa: An impulse-control condition involving a recurrent urge to steal items not needed for personal use or monetary value
ds: Term coined by Swiss physician Andre Matthey in 1816
pr: Affects fewer than 1 in 100 people, more often diagnosed in women
her: Some genetic component, often overlapping with mood disorders
dl: Can create legal and relationship consequences distinct from ordinary theft motivations

fa: A rare impulse-control condition involving a recurrent urge to deliberately set fires for a sense of relief or gratification
ds: Term coined in early 19th-century French psychiatry
pr: Considered rare, affecting a very small share of the population
her: Limited research; some overlap with impulse-control conditions
dl: Can create significant legal and safety consequences distinct from ordinary fire-setting motivations

fa: Involves recurrent, sudden episodes of impulsive anger disproportionate to the situation that triggered them
ds: Introduced as a diagnosis in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 2-3 in 100 adults over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Sudden anger episodes can strain relationships, employment and legal standing

fa: Involves deliberately producing or exaggerating symptoms of illness without an obvious external reward
ds: Named after Baron Munchausen by physician Richard Asher in 1951
pr: Considered rare, though exact figures are difficult to establish
her: Not clearly hereditary; primarily psychological in origin
dl: Repeated medical-seeking behavior can lead to unnecessary procedures and strained relationships with healthcare providers

fa: A common learning difference primarily affecting accurate and fluent word reading, unrelated to overall intelligence
ds: Term coined by ophthalmologist Rudolf Berlin in 1887
pr: Affects roughly 5-10 in 100 people
her: Substantial genetic component, among the most heritable learning differences
dl: Can require additional support and time for reading-heavy schoolwork and tasks

fa: A learning difference primarily affecting the ability to understand numbers and perform mathematical calculations
ds: Term introduced in the 1940s, gaining wider clinical use from the 1970s onward
pr: Affects roughly 3-6 in 100 people
her: Moderate to substantial genetic component
dl: Can make everyday tasks like managing money or telling time more challenging

fa: A learning difference affecting handwriting and the physical act of writing
ds: Described in educational and neurological literature through the 20th century
pr: Prevalence not precisely established; frequently co-occurs with other learning differences
her: Some genetic component, often overlapping with dyslexia
dl: Can make handwriting tasks slower and more physically tiring

fa: Commonly known as stuttering, involves disruptions in the normal fluency of speech
ds: Stuttering described since antiquity; modern clinical study began in the 19th century
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 100 adults, with a higher rate in young children that often resolves
her: Substantial genetic component
dl: Can affect confidence in speaking situations at school, work and socially

fa: Involves a persistent pattern of angry mood and defiant behavior toward authority figures
ds: Introduced as a diagnosis in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 3 in 100 children
her: Moderate genetic component alongside environmental factors
dl: Persistent conflict with authority figures can affect school and family relationships

fa: Involves difficulty forming healthy attachments, typically linked to early childhood neglect or inconsistent caregiving
ds: Introduced as a diagnosis in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Considered rare in the general population, more common among children with early institutional care
her: Not strongly hereditary; primarily linked to early caregiving experiences
dl: Can affect a child's ability to form trusting relationships with caregivers

fa: Involves an impaired ability to stop or control alcohol use despite negative consequences
ds: Concept of habitual drinking as a medical condition dates to the 18th century; modern criteria refined through the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Affects roughly 5-6 in 100 adults globally in a given year
her: Substantial genetic component alongside environmental and social factors
dl: Can affect work performance, relationships and physical health over time

fa: Involves persistent, problematic gambling behavior causing significant distress or impairment
ds: First recognized as a psychiatric diagnosis in the DSM-III, 1980
pr: Affects roughly 1-2 in 100 adults
her: Moderate genetic component, overlapping with other impulse-control conditions
dl: Can lead to significant financial, legal and relationship consequences

fa: A condition proposed for further study involving impaired control over gaming despite negative consequences
ds: Proposed for further study in the DSM-5, 2013
pr: Prevalence estimates vary widely across studies and regions
her: Limited research so far; likely overlaps with other impulse-control conditions
dl: Can displace sleep, schoolwork or in-person social activities when severe

fa: Involves repeated disturbing dreams that cause significant distress or interfere with sleep
ds: Described in sleep medicine literature through the 20th century
pr: Occasional nightmares are nearly universal; the disorder itself is less common
her: Moderate genetic component, often overlapping with anxiety conditions
dl: Frequent disturbing dreams can lead to fear of sleep and daytime fatigue

fa: Also called somnambulism, involves walking or performing other activities while still mostly asleep
ds: Documented since antiquity; studied scientifically from the 19th century onward
pr: Affects roughly 1-4 in 100 adults, more common in children
her: Moderate genetic component, often running in families
dl: Usually low daily impact, though safety precautions are often recommended

fa: Involves episodes of intense fear during sleep accompanied by screaming or thrashing
ds: Documented since antiquity; formally studied in modern sleep medicine from the 20th century
pr: Affects roughly 1-6 in 100 children, less common in adults
her: Moderate genetic component
dl: Episodes are usually forgotten by morning and have limited long-term daily impact

fa: Involves repeated involuntary urination, most commonly discussed in the context of childhood bedwetting
ds: Documented throughout medical history; modern clinical criteria refined in the 20th century
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 10 children at age 5, decreasing with age
her: Substantial genetic component, often running in families
dl: Can affect a child's confidence around sleepovers and school activities

fa: Involves persistent feelings of being detached from one's own body or surroundings
ds: Term 'depersonalization' introduced by philosopher Ludovic Dugas in 1898
pr: Affects roughly 1-2 in 100 people over a lifetime
her: Moderate genetic component, often linked to anxiety and stress
dl: Persistent detachment can make everyday experiences feel unsettling or dreamlike

fa: Involves numerous periods of mild mood elevation and mild depressive symptoms that don't meet the full criteria for bipolar disorder
ds: Concept described by psychiatrist Karl Kahlbaum in 1882
pr: Affects less than 1 in 100 people
her: Moderate to substantial genetic component, related to bipolar disorder
dl: Frequent mild mood swings can subtly affect relationships and daily consistency

fa: A chronic condition involving disruptions in thinking, perception and behavior, often including hallucinations or delusions
ds: Term coined by psychiatrist Eugen Bleuler in 1908, building on earlier work by Emil Kraepelin
pr: Affects roughly 1 in 100 people worldwide over a lifetime
her: Substantial genetic component, among the most heritable psychiatric conditions
dl: Can significantly affect perception, thinking and daily functioning, with impact varying widely by individual and treatment
